documents.subject: math
documents.text: Algebra I introduces linear and quadratic functions, solving equations and inequalities, systems of equations, polynomials, and data analysis. It is the foundational high school math course. Prerequisite: none. Credit: 1.0. Grade level: 9.

documents.subject: math
documents.text: Geometry covers points, lines, planes, congruence, similarity, right-triangle trigonometry, circles, coordinate geometry, and proofs. Prerequisite: Algebra I. Credit: 1.0. Grade levels: 9-10. The course typically follows Algebra I in the sequence and precedes Algebra II.

documents.subject: math
documents.text: Algebra II extends the concepts of Algebra I to include quadratic functions, polynomial functions, rational functions, exponential and logarithmic functions, sequences and series, and an introduction to probability. Prerequisite: Algebra I and Geometry. Credit: 1.0. Grade levels: 10-11.

documents.subject: math
documents.text: Precalculus integrates algebraic and trigonometric concepts in preparation for calculus. Topics include functions and their properties, exponential and logarithmic functions, trigonometric functions, polar coordinates, vectors, and an introduction to limits. Prerequisite: Algebra II. Credit: 1.0. Grade levels: 11-12.

documents.subject: math
documents.text: AP Calculus AB covers the topics of the first semester of college calculus: limits, derivatives, integrals, and the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus. Students take the AP exam in May for potential college credit. Prerequisite: Precalculus with a grade of B or higher. Credit: 1.0. Grade levels: 11-12.

documents.subject: math
documents.text: AP Calculus BC covers the equivalent of two semesters of college calculus, including all AB topics plus sequences and series, parametric/polar/vector functions, and additional integration techniques. Prerequisite: AP Calculus AB or Precalculus with a grade of A. Credit: 1.0. Grade level: 12.

documents.subject: math
documents.text: AP Statistics introduces data analysis, probability, and statistical inference. Topics include exploring data, designing studies, anticipating patterns, and drawing conclusions. Recommended for students pursuing social sciences, life sciences, or business. Prerequisite: Algebra II. Credit: 1.0. Grade levels: 11-12.

documents.subject: math
documents.text: Algebra I is the first high school math course, covering linear equations, inequalities, functions, systems of equations, exponential functions, and polynomial operations. Prerequisite: Pre-Algebra or 8th grade math. Credit: 1.0. Grades: 8-9.

documents.subject: math
documents.text: Geometry develops spatial reasoning through the study of points, lines, angles, polygons, circles, and three-dimensional figures. Topics include congruence, similarity, transformations, coordinate geometry, and proofs. Prerequisite: Algebra I. Credit: 1.0. Grades: 9-10.

documents.subject: math
documents.text: Algebra II expands algebraic reasoning to include complex numbers, quadratic and polynomial functions, rational expressions, exponential and logarithmic functions, and trigonometry. Prerequisite: Algebra I and Geometry. Credit: 1.0. Grades: 10-11.

documents.subject: math
documents.text: Precalculus bridges Algebra II and Calculus. Topics include advanced functions, matrices, conic sections, trigonometry, sequences and series, and an introduction to limits. Students develop the analytical skills needed for AP Calculus. Prerequisite: Algebra II. Credit: 1.0. Grades: 11-12.

documents.subject: math
documents.text: AP Calculus AB is equivalent to a first-semester college calculus course. Topics include limits, derivatives, integrals, and the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus. Students may earn college credit via the AP exam. Prerequisite: Precalculus. Credit: 1.0. Grades: 11-12.
